content:
Why successful AI adoption depends on the people the last forty years of management stopped promoting

7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

There is a survey result making the rounds that ought to stop a boardroom cold, and won’t. Asked by Pew last year whether artificial intelligence would have a positive effect on how people do their jobs over the next two decades, How the U.S. Public and AI Experts View Artificial Intelligence identified that seventy-three percent of AI experts said yes, but among the public, only twenty-three percent agreed.
A fifty-point gap separates the people building the technology from the people expected to work alongside it. Add the rest of the recent numbers — nearly half of Gen Z workers convinced the risks outweigh the benefits, roughly one in five workers worldwide confident their job is safe, seventy percent of Americans opposed to a data center anywhere near them — and you have a workforce that has reached a verdict well ahead of the people instructing it to feel otherwise.
The standard reading of this gap treats it as a communications problem. Employees are afraid; leadership must reassure them; the chief information officer is handed the job of bridging the distance between the C-suite’s enthusiasm and the shop floor’s dread. This is the framing in nearly every trade article on the subject, and it is wrong in a way that guarantees the failures it is meant to prevent.
The gap is not a misunderstanding to be managed. It is an accurate reading, by the people closest to the work, of an effort being planned by people who are not.

A deficiency forty years in the making
To see why, it helps to be precise about what successful AI adoption actually requires, because almost nothing about it resembles the procurement decision it is usually mistaken for. The value a capable AI system creates does not live in the obvious, countable places. It lives in the friction inside a workflow that no one has bothered to name, in the judgment a senior person applies a hundred times a day without being able to explain how, in the handoff between two departments that everyone has quietly learned to route around.
This is the tacit layer of an organization — the knowledge that makes the work work, and that exists nowhere in writing because it was never written down. It is acquired only by proximity, over years, by people doing the thing.
None of that is visible from the financial view of a company. The balance sheet shows cost centers and headcount. It does not show where the real friction sits or where applied judgment is quietly carrying the operation. And here is the difficulty that the cheerful adoption literature steps around: a great many of the people now planning these transitions can read an organization only as a balance sheet, because that is the only way they were ever required to read one.
That is not an accident of individual character. It is the designed output of a selection process that has been running for two generations. The shift that produced it is well documented and easy to date — Milton Friedman’s 1970 declaration that the social responsibility of business is to increase its profits, the agency theory that followed, the regulatory blessing of stock buybacks in 1982, the steady replacement of the long horizon with the quarterly one.
The point is not to relitigate that history on moral grounds. The point is narrower and, for present purposes, far more damaging. A culture that rewards share-price growth above all else selects for executives who are fluent in the things that move share price: cost extraction, financial engineering, narrative for the analyst call. It does not select for, develop, or even recognize the executive who understands in genuine depth how a particular organization produces value.
That competency is invisible to the fitness function. What is invisible does not get measured, what does not get measured does not get promoted, and what is not promoted eventually stops being produced, because the people coming up watch what is rewarded and aim themselves accordingly.
This is not a new charge. Hayes and Abernathy laid it out in the Harvard Business Review in 1980, in an essay titled “Managing Our Way to Economic Decline.” Their target was a management class that had become a kind of pseudo-profession — people credentialed in finance and law who believed they could run any enterprise precisely because they understood none of them in particular, and who therefore optimized whatever was measurable and neglected whatever was not.
They were explaining the collapse of American manufacturing. They were also, it turns out, describing the precise deficiency that a technology arriving forty-five years later would expose more brutally than anything in between.
Why this technology is the unforgiving test
Earlier waves of technology could be bought and bolted on. You licensed the software, you trained the staff, you booked the efficiency. AI does not behave this way, because the gains it offers are not sitting in the legible places where they can simply be purchased. They are buried in the tacit layer — and an executive who knows his organization only through its numbers literally cannot locate them.
He can see headcount in a cost center. He cannot see the senior analyst whose unwritten judgment holds three brittle processes together. So he deploys the technology where it is visible rather than where it is valuable, which means he deploys it as a substitute for people in the places he can count, and misses entirely the places where it might have multiplied what his best people can do.
The tell is the blanket mandate. When a company hands every employee a key performance indicator instructing them to use AI, it is not exercising judgment about where the technology belongs. It is confessing that it cannot make that judgment, because making it would require knowing the work in detail no one in the planning room possesses. The mandate is what you reach for when you cannot make the targeted call. It is a substitute for understanding, and the workforce reads it as exactly that.
This also explains why “just hire the expertise” is a trap rather than a solution. Buying the missing knowledge requires enough of your own to know whom to trust, which consultant is right when two of them disagree, and which proposed redesign is sound rather than merely convincing. You cannot outsource a judgment you are unequipped to evaluate.
You can only outsource it to whoever sells it most persuasively — which is how the original deficiency reproduces itself one level down, now wearing a slide deck.

The people who are not in the room
Which brings us to the requirement that the entire adoption discussion has managed to leave out. The knowledge needed to plan one of these transitions well already exists inside almost every organization attempting one. It belongs to the real domain experts — the senior practitioners who know how the work actually gets done, where it breaks, where judgment is silently carrying the load, and where a capable tool would genuinely lift the people around it rather than merely replace them.
These are the only people who can tell the difference between where AI is valuable and where it is just visible. They are also, with grim reliability, the people not invited to plan the transition.
There are reasons for that, and the reasons are themselves part of the problem. The deep expert is frequently unreadable to the very measurement systems that decide who counts as leadership material. The trait that makes him valuable — total absorption in the substance of the work, often paired with open contempt for the narrative layer — is exactly the trait that keeps him from scoring well on the portable, standardized instruments that human resources built over those same decades to identify high potential.
A measurement system that cannot see something will, given enough time, select it out and then report that the pipeline is healthy, because everyone in the pipeline scores well on the things the system can see. The expert did not fail to rise. He was structurally invisible to the apparatus that does the rising.
So the prescription is not the soft one the trade press keeps offering. It is not more town halls, more sentiment surveys, more carefully worded reassurance. Those treat the symptom.
The actual requirement is that the people who hold the tacit knowledge of how the organization produces value be brought into the design of the transition as participants, not as subjects to be surveyed afterward. Not consulted for comfort. Seated at the table where the decisions about what to automate, what to augment, and what to leave alone are actually made — because they are the only people in the building who know which is which.
This is harder than it sounds, and the difficulty is the same loop closing on itself. Recognizing those people, and trusting their judgment over the more legible confidence of an outside vendor, requires exactly the competency that the last forty years declined to select for.
The organizations that come through this well will not be the ones that buy the most or mandate the hardest. They will be the ones whose leadership can finally see what their own measurement systems were built to overlook: that the knowledge the moment demands has been in the building all along, held by the people management spent two generations learning not to promote.
The workforce already senses this. That fifty-point gap is not fear of the machine. It is the accurate intuition of people who understand their own work watching it be planned by people who do not.
The way to close the gap is not to talk the real experts out of what they can plainly see. It is to put them in the room where the decisions are made.

Author’s note
By the late 1970s I was a branch manager at a software consulting company. I had a front-row seat to what I called the MBA disease: the replacement of managers having domain expertise by managers with MBAs — the replacement of qualification by quantification.
By the mid 1980’s I had been recruited by Jack Welch to turn around a software company that GE had acquired. That experience solidified my opinion that general business accounting expertise might be able to optimize a few quarterly reports, but it did little to increase the real value of a company — only its short-term share price.
